Bold text indicates deities enshrined at this shrine, and red text indicates female figures. All are listed according to the official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html]. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:90%}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01| 01='''{{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}'''|02={{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|03={{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|04=}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||:}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01||||02| 01={{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Mishima Mizokui||Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||!|||||!}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01|y|~|~|02| 01={{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|{{color|#FC4E6B|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0||||||,|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|border=0|||||01||!|||!| 01='''Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto'''}} {{familytree|border=0|01|y|~|~|~|02||!| 01=(1) {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}|02='''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|{{color|#FC4E6B|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||01|y|~|~|~|~|~|02| 01=(2) {{jalink|Emperor Suizei|Q313321}}|02='''{{jalink|Isuzu-yori-hime|Q10881557|{{color|#FC4E6B|Isuzu Yorihime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree/end}}
The current enshrined deities are the following six kamiThe list of enshrined deities follows the official website’s “Shrine Grounds Guide” [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129210623/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub1.html].. ; Principal deities * '''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}''' (たまくしひめのみこと) – Mother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * '''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと) – Empress of Emperor Jimmu ; Co-enshrined deities * {{jalink|Mishima Mizokui||Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}} (みぞくいみみのみこと) – Grandfather of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto}} (あめのひかたくしひかたのみこと) – Brother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanō-no-Mikoto}} (すさのおのみこと) – Ancestor of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} (あめのこやねのみこと) – Ancestor of the {{jalink|Fujiwara clan|Q641628}} === History === === Foundation === The date of foundation is unknown. According to shrine tradition, it was founded around the reign of the 10th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html History and Sacred Treasures] (official website).. As noted in the section on enshrined deities, this shrine worships the lineage of {{jalink|Kamotake-tsunumi-no-Mikoto||Mishima Mizokui Mimi}}, and its relationship with traditions found in the {{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}} and {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} has been pointed out. In the Kojiki account of Emperor Jimmu, {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Q10936701|Ōmononushi-no-Kami}} falls in love with {{jalink|Seyadatara-hime}} (Tamaki-hime), the daughter of Mishima Mizokui, transforms into a vermilion-painted arrow, and strikes the maiden’s genitals. Though startled, she places the arrow by her bed, whereupon it turns into a handsome man, and from their union is born Futo-tatara Isuzu-ki-hime-no-Mikoto (Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto). In the Age of the Gods volume of the Nihon Shoki, it is written that Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was the child of the Ōmiwa deity, and that {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} transformed into an eight-fathom crocodile and visited Mishima Mizokui-hime (Tamaki-hime), producing Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, who later became the consort of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. A similar account appears in the Nihon Shoki entry for the 16th day of the 8th month of the year of Emperor Jimmu’s accession, where Tamaki-hime is described as the daughter of Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto. Based on these texts, and noting that within the shrine precincts Kotoshironushi-no-Kami is enshrined rather than Ōmononushi-no-Kami, some theories point to interaction between the local earth-deity lineage of the {{jalink|Kamo clan}} that worshipped Kotoshironushi and the Mishima power group{{efn|Although Kotoshironushi is described as a child of Ōkuninushi of Izumo, one theory in *Nihon no Kamigami* suggests that Kotoshironushi was originally of Kamo lineage rather than Izumo.}}. In the surrounding area remain shrines such as {{jalink|Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295}} and {{jalink|Kamo Shrine (Takatsuki City)|Q11675604|Kamo Shrine}}, as well as place names like Kamomura and Kamobayashi, suggesting the influence of either the heavenly or earthly Kamo lineage. {{要出典範囲|There is also a theory that these interactions were later reconstructed into the myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, emphasizing Izumo deities as described above|date=November 2020}}. From the view that “mizo” symbolizes water and “mimi” represents length, another theory holds that this lineage was involved in irrigation related to the {{jalink|Ai River|Q11450512}} system. === Historical outline === According to shrine tradition, the area once contained the {{jalink|field allotment|Q11381648}} of {{jalink|Prince Abo|Q11657231}}, son of {{jalink|Emperor Heijō|Q353558}}. During a drought in the {{jalink|Tencho|Q1373138}} era ({{jalink|824|Q30353}}–{{jalink|834|Q30381}}), the prince petitioned the court and received a mirror, with which he prayed for rain; heavy rain then fell“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The sacred treasure known as the “Dawn Sacred Mirror” (a bronze mirror with two deities and two beasts) is said to be that mirror. In the {{jalink|Engi Shiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, compiled in {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed as “Mizokui Shrine, Shimashimo District, Settsu Province,” and is thus counted as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. In ancient times, there was an “Upper Shrine” about 500 meters to the northeast (present-day Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City) where Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was enshrined, while the current precincts were known as the “Lower Shrine,” enshrining Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto*Nihon no Kamigami*, entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The period when they were separated is unclear, but medieval documents from {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1 ({{jalink|1441|Q6402}}) mention place names of Upper Shrine and Lower Shrine. The Lower Shrine is considered the original Shikinaisha, and until their merger in Meiji 42, rituals were conducted at both shrines. Shiga Tsuyoshi, in *Research on Shikinaisha*, suggests that in ancient times the Lower Shrine’s Tamakushi-hime served the Upper Shrine’s Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, but after Isuzu-hime became Empress of Emperor Jimmu, the mother deity gained prominence and the Lower Shrine was elevated to Shikinaisha status. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunmei|Q1193544}} 9 ({{jalink|1477|Q6614}}) by the local lord Mizokui Hyōgo-no-suke Sadanobu, and again during the {{jalink|Bunroku|Q1373039}} era ({{jalink|1592|Q6769}}–{{jalink|1596|Q6785}}) by Hasegawa Shikibu-shōyū. The current buildings date from {{jalink|Kanpō|Q1145798}} 2 ({{jalink|1742|Q7575}}), constructed by Komeya Uemon and {{jalink|Sawano Tsuru|Q7428583|Komeya Kibei}}. Until the Meiji Restoration, the precincts also contained a Shingon temple, Fudō-in Myōren-ji, as the shrine-temple complex. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5), under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}, and in {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40) it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and paper offerings. In {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), three deities enshrined at Mizokui Upper Shrine east of the Ai River—Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto—were merged here. In the same year, the Tenman Shrine (Sugawara no Michizane) from Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the grounds, and in {{jalink|1910|Q2075}} (Meiji 43) the Arakami Shrine (Kagutsuchi-no-Kami, Nakatsuhiko-no-Kami, Nakatsuhime-no-Kami) from Megaki was also relocated to the precincts. In {{jalink|1941|Q5231}} (Shōwa 16), the shrine was promoted to {{jalink|Fusha}} status. === Shrine grounds === File:Mizokui-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Mizokui-jinja haiden.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Mizokui-jinja entrance.JPG|Entrance to the grounds === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Mizokui 006.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Kotoshironushi Shrine]] === Associated shrines === * Kotoshironushi Shrine ** Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami||Kagu-tsuchi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Okutsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Okutsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Mitsuhanome-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Haniyasubiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Haniyasubime-no-Kami}} :: Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto is the husband deity of Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto. During the Meiji period, the deities of Megaki Village were merged here. === Minor shrines === * Amaterasu Ōmikami Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} * Uke-mochi Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Uke Mochi-no-Kami|Q2280323}} * Tajikarao Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao||Tajikarao-no-Kami}} * Konohana Sakuya-hime Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Konohana Sakuya-hime||Konohana Sakuya-hime-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|Shinatsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} On April 23, 1909 (Meiji 42), the Tenman Shrine of Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the Ichikishima Shrine. === Festivals === * Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Tondo Festival (January 15) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Inari Festival (second Sunday of April) * Ancestral Spirit Festival (Ryōsen Festival) (April 13 → April 11) – Noh performances were once dedicated. Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association * Arakami Festival (third Sunday of April) * Spring Grand Festival (May 5) * Autumn Grand Festival (October 14) * Shichi-Go-San (November 15) * Fire-Quelling Festival (December 10) * Great Purification Festival (December 31) In the past, based on the episode in the Nihon Shoki (Age of the Gods, upper volume) in which Kotoshironushi-no-Kami visited Mishima Mizokui-hime, a joint procession was held on the same day with Mishima Kamo Shrine, which also enshrines Kotoshironushi-no-Kami“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mishima Kamo Shrine.. === Sacred treasures === * Dawn Sacred Mirror According to shrine tradition, this mirror was used by Prince Abo during prayers for rain. It is a bronze mirror from China’s Han dynasty, decorated with two deities and two beasts. === Excavated pottery === In 1939 (Shōwa 14), during river improvement works on the Ai River, large quantities of {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}} and {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}} were excavated from land adjacent to the former Mizokui Upper Shrine, and records show they were kept at Mizokui ShrineAmbo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association. The site of the former Upper Shrine and its surroundings constitute the “'''Mizokui Archaeological Site'''.” In excavations conducted in 1997 (Heisei 9) by the {{jalink|Public Interest Incorporated Foundation|Q40734470}} {{jalink|Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center|Q11425913}} (formerly the Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Research Center), remains were discovered including {{jalink|small-plot paddy fields|Q86743587}} from the {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, artifacts from the {{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} and {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and square-section structural remains believed to be the former Mizokui Upper Shrine from the latter half of the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}[https://www.occh.or.jp/?s=data/setumeikai Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center] 1997. === Related sites === [[File:Mizokui 101.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Monument marking the former Mizokui Upper Shrine]] * Former site of Mizokui Upper Shrine (Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City, {{ウィキ座標|34|49|02.64|N|135|35|25.28|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|location|name=Upper Shrine Site (monument)}}) : The site of the former Upper Shrine, marked by a stone monument. It was erected in April 1965 (Shōwa 40) when Naniwa Commercial High School built an athletic field on the site. * {{jalink|Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295}} : A shrine in Takatsuki City located across the Ai River. The crest of Mishima Kamo Shrine can be seen on stone lanterns within Mizokui Shrine’s grounds. According to tradition, portable shrine processions from Mishima Kamo Shrine to Mizokui Shrine began in Genroku 10 (1697) and continued until the Meiji period. === Visitor information === ; Address * 9-21 Isuzuchō, {{jalink|Ibaraki City|Q502403}}, Osaka Prefecture ; Access * From {{jalink|Ibaraki Station|Q1072931}} ({{jalink|West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|JR West}}) East Exit or {{jalink|Ibaraki-shi Station|Q3969806}} ({{jalink|Hankyu Corporation|Q473058}}) South Exit, take the {{jalink|Kintetsu Bus|Q18636319}} (Route 84 Kuwata–Hirata Loop) and get off at “Kuwata-chō” bus {{jalink|October 14|Q2922}} (Autumn Festival) |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Mizokui Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} {{Coordinate list}} '''Mizokui Shrine''' (みぞくいじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Ibaraki City|Q502403}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine ranking}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Fusha}}. It is an independent shrine. Due to damage from the {{jalink|2018 Northern Osaka Earthquake}}, the protective buildings of the {{jalink|subsidiary shrines}} were rebuilt in a modern style. The former chief priest’s residence adjacent to the shrine was removed, and the shrine office was also rebuilt. On the 1st and 2nd of each month, amulets are distributed, and thanks to the efforts of the parishioners, the shrine’s rituals continue to be maintained. === Overview === Enshrined deities
Simplified diagram of relationships among the enshrined deities
Solid lines indicate direct children; dotted vertical lines indicate abbreviated multiple generations; dotted horizontal lines indicate marriage relationships; numbers indicate the order of imperial accession. Bold text indicates deities enshrined at this shrine, and red text indicates female figures. All are listed according to the official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html]. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:90%}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01| 01='''{{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}'''|02={{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|03={{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|04=}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||:}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01||||02| 01={{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Mishima Mizokui||Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||!|||||!}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01|y|~|~|02| 01={{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|{{color|#FC4E6B|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0||||||,|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|border=0|||||01||!|||!| 01='''Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto'''}} {{familytree|border=0|01|y|~|~|~|02||!| 01=(1) {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}|02='''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|{{color|#FC4E6B|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||01|y|~|~|~|~|~|02| 01=(2) {{jalink|Emperor Suizei|Q313321}}|02='''{{jalink|Isuzu-yori-hime|Q10881557|{{color|#FC4E6B|Isuzu Yorihime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree/end}}
The current enshrined deities are the following six kamiThe list of enshrined deities follows the official website’s “Shrine Grounds Guide” [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129210623/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub1.html].. ; Principal deities * '''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}''' (たまくしひめのみこと) – Mother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * '''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと) – Empress of Emperor Jimmu ; Co-enshrined deities * {{jalink|Mishima Mizokui||Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}} (みぞくいみみのみこと) – Grandfather of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto}} (あめのひかたくしひかたのみこと) – Brother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Hayasusanō-no-Mikoto}} (すさのおのみこと) – Ancestor of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} (あめのこやねのみこと) – Ancestor of the {{jalink|Fujiwara clan|Q641628}} === History === === Foundation === The date of foundation is unknown. According to shrine tradition, it was founded around the reign of the 10th emperor, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html History and Sacred Treasures] (official website).. As noted in the section on enshrined deities, this shrine worships the lineage of {{jalink|Kamotake-tsunumi-no-Mikoto||Mishima Mizokui Mimi}}, and its relationship with traditions found in the {{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}} and {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} has been pointed out. In the Kojiki account of Emperor Jimmu, {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Q10936701|Ōmononushi-no-Kami}} falls in love with {{jalink|Seyadatara-hime}} (Tamaki-hime), the daughter of Mishima Mizokui, transforms into a vermilion-painted arrow, and strikes the maiden’s genitals. Though startled, she places the arrow by her bed, whereupon it turns into a handsome man, and from their union is born Futo-tatara Isuzu-ki-hime-no-Mikoto (Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto). In the Age of the Gods volume of the Nihon Shoki, it is written that Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was the child of the Ōmiwa deity, and that {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} transformed into an eight-fathom crocodile and visited Mishima Mizokui-hime (Tamaki-hime), producing Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, who later became the consort of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. A similar account appears in the Nihon Shoki entry for the 16th day of the 8th month of the year of Emperor Jimmu’s accession, where Tamaki-hime is described as the daughter of Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto. Based on these texts, and noting that within the shrine precincts Kotoshironushi-no-Kami is enshrined rather than Ōmononushi-no-Kami, some theories point to interaction between the local earth-deity lineage of the {{jalink|Kamo clan}} that worshipped Kotoshironushi and the Mishima power group{{efn|Although Kotoshironushi is described as a child of Ōkuninushi of Izumo, one theory in *Nihon no Kamigami* suggests that Kotoshironushi was originally of Kamo lineage rather than Izumo.}}. In the surrounding area remain shrines such as {{jalink|Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295}} and {{jalink|Kamo Shrine (Takatsuki City)|Q11675604|Kamo Shrine}}, as well as place names like Kamomura and Kamobayashi, suggesting the influence of either the heavenly or earthly Kamo lineage. {{要出典範囲|There is also a theory that these interactions were later reconstructed into the myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, emphasizing Izumo deities as described above|date=November 2020}}. From the view that “mizo” symbolizes water and “mimi” represents length, another theory holds that this lineage was involved in irrigation related to the {{jalink|Ai River|Q11450512}} system. === Historical outline === According to shrine tradition, the area once contained the {{jalink|field allotment|Q11381648}} of {{jalink|Prince Abo|Q11657231}}, son of {{jalink|Emperor Heijō|Q353558}}. During a drought in the {{jalink|Tencho|Q1373138}} era ({{jalink|824|Q30353}}–{{jalink|834|Q30381}}), the prince petitioned the court and received a mirror, with which he prayed for rain; heavy rain then fell“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The sacred treasure known as the “Dawn Sacred Mirror” (a bronze mirror with two deities and two beasts) is said to be that mirror. In the {{jalink|Engi Shiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, compiled in {{jalink|Engi|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed as “Mizokui Shrine, Shimashimo District, Settsu Province,” and is thus counted as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. In ancient times, there was an “Upper Shrine” about 500 meters to the northeast (present-day Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City) where Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was enshrined, while the current precincts were known as the “Lower Shrine,” enshrining Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto*Nihon no Kamigami*, entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The period when they were separated is unclear, but medieval documents from {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1 ({{jalink|1441|Q6402}}) mention place names of Upper Shrine and Lower Shrine. The Lower Shrine is considered the original Shikinaisha, and until their merger in Meiji 42, rituals were conducted at both shrines. Shiga Tsuyoshi, in *Research on Shikinaisha*, suggests that in ancient times the Lower Shrine’s Tamakushi-hime served the Upper Shrine’s Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, but after Isuzu-hime became Empress of Emperor Jimmu, the mother deity gained prominence and the Lower Shrine was elevated to Shikinaisha status. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunmei|Q1193544}} 9 ({{jalink|1477|Q6614}}) by the local lord Mizokui Hyōgo-no-suke Sadanobu, and again during the {{jalink|Bunroku|Q1373039}} era ({{jalink|1592|Q6769}}–{{jalink|1596|Q6785}}) by Hasegawa Shikibu-shōyū. The current buildings date from {{jalink|Kanpō|Q1145798}} 2 ({{jalink|1742|Q7575}}), constructed by Komeya Uemon and {{jalink|Sawano Tsuru|Q7428583|Komeya Kibei}}. Until the Meiji Restoration, the precincts also contained a Shingon temple, Fudō-in Myōren-ji, as the shrine-temple complex. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5), under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}, and in {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40) it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and paper offerings. In {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), three deities enshrined at Mizokui Upper Shrine east of the Ai River—Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto—were merged here. In the same year, the Tenman Shrine (Sugawara no Michizane) from Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the grounds, and in {{jalink|1910|Q2075}} (Meiji 43) the Arakami Shrine (Kagutsuchi-no-Kami, Nakatsuhiko-no-Kami, Nakatsuhime-no-Kami) from Megaki was also relocated to the precincts. In {{jalink|1941|Q5231}} (Shōwa 16), the shrine was promoted to {{jalink|Fusha}} status. === Shrine grounds === File:Mizokui-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Mizokui-jinja haiden.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Mizokui-jinja entrance.JPG|Entrance to the grounds === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Mizokui 006.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Kotoshironushi Shrine]] === Associated shrines === * Kotoshironushi Shrine ** Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami||Kagu-tsuchi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Okutsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Okutsuhime-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Mitsuhanome-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Haniyasubiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Haniyasubime-no-Kami}} :: Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto is the husband deity of Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto. During the Meiji period, the deities of Megaki Village were merged here. === Minor shrines === * Amaterasu Ōmikami Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami}}, {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} * Uke-mochi Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Uke Mochi-no-Kami|Q2280323}} * Tajikarao Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao||Tajikarao-no-Kami}} * Konohana Sakuya-hime Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Konohana Sakuya-hime||Konohana Sakuya-hime-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|Shinatsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} On April 23, 1909 (Meiji 42), the Tenman Shrine of Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the Ichikishima Shrine. === Festivals === * Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Tondo Festival (January 15) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Inari Festival (second Sunday of April) * Ancestral Spirit Festival (Ryōsen Festival) (April 13 → April 11) – Noh performances were once dedicated. Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association * Arakami Festival (third Sunday of April) * Spring Grand Festival (May 5) * Autumn Grand Festival (October 14) * Shichi-Go-San (November 15) * Fire-Quelling Festival (December 10) * Great Purification Festival (December 31) In the past, based on the episode in the Nihon Shoki (Age of the Gods, upper volume) in which Kotoshironushi-no-Kami visited Mishima Mizokui-hime, a joint procession was held on the same day with Mishima Kamo Shrine, which also enshrines Kotoshironushi-no-Kami“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mishima Kamo Shrine.. === Sacred treasures === * Dawn Sacred Mirror According to shrine tradition, this mirror was used by Prince Abo during prayers for rain. It is a bronze mirror from China’s Han dynasty, decorated with two deities and two beasts. === Excavated pottery === In 1939 (Shōwa 14), during river improvement works on the Ai River, large quantities of {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}} and {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}} were excavated from land adjacent to the former Mizokui Upper Shrine, and records show they were kept at Mizokui ShrineAmbo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association. The site of the former Upper Shrine and its surroundings constitute the “'''Mizokui Archaeological Site'''.” In excavations conducted in 1997 (Heisei 9) by the {{jalink|Public Interest Incorporated Foundation|Q40734470}} {{jalink|Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center|Q11425913}} (formerly the Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Research Center), remains were discovered including {{jalink|small-plot paddy fields|Q86743587}} from the {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, artifacts from the {{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} and {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and square-section structural remains believed to be the former Mizokui Upper Shrine from the latter half of the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}[https://www.occh.or.jp/?s=data/setumeikai Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center] 1997. === Related sites === [[File:Mizokui 101.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Monument marking the former Mizokui Upper Shrine]] * Former site of Mizokui Upper Shrine (Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City, {{ウィキ座標|34|49|02.64|N|135|35|25.28|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|location|name=Upper Shrine Site (monument)}}) : The site of the former Upper Shrine, marked by a stone monument. It was erected in April 1965 (Shōwa 40) when Naniwa Commercial High School built an athletic field on the site. * {{jalink|Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295}} : A shrine in Takatsuki City located across the Ai River. The crest of Mishima Kamo Shrine can be seen on stone lanterns within Mizokui Shrine’s grounds. According to tradition, portable shrine processions from Mishima Kamo Shrine to Mizokui Shrine began in Genroku 10 (1697) and continued until the Meiji period. === Visitor information === ; Address * 9-21 Isuzuchō, {{jalink|Ibaraki City|Q502403}}, Osaka Prefecture ; Access * From {{jalink|Ibaraki Station|Q1072931}} ({{jalink|West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|JR West}}) East Exit or {{jalink|Ibaraki-shi Station|Q3969806}} ({{jalink|Hankyu Corporation|Q473058}}) South Exit, take the {{jalink|Kintetsu Bus|Q18636319}} (Route 84 Kuwata–Hirata Loop) and get off at “Kuwata-chō” bus stop; about a 4-minute walk east after disembarking. === Notes === ; Annotations ; References {{reflist}} === Further reading === * Inoue Masao, *Complete Gazetteer of Osaka Prefecture* (1922) {{ill|lt=October 14|Q2922|WD=Q2922}} (Autumn Festival) |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Mizokui Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} {{Coordinate list}} '''Mizokui Shrine''' (みぞくいじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}} it was classified as a {{ill||lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}}. It is an independent shrine. Due to damage from the {{ill||lt=2018 Northern Osaka Earthquake|UNKNOWN}}, the protective buildings of the {{ill||lt=subsidiary shrines|UNKNOWN}} were rebuilt in a modern style. The former chief priest’s residence adjacent to the shrine was removed, and the shrine office was also rebuilt. On the 1st and 2nd of each month, amulets are distributed, and thanks to the efforts of the parishioners, the shrine’s rituals continue to be maintained. === Overview === Enshrined deities

The current enshrined deities are the following six kamiThe list of enshrined deities follows the official website’s “Shrine Grounds Guide” [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129210623/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub1.html].. ; Principal deities * '''{{ill|lt=Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}''' (たまくしひめのみこと) – Mother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * '''{{ill|lt=Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|WD=Q5353793|lt=Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと) – Empress of Emperor Jimmu ; Co-enshrined deities * {{ill|lt=Mishima Mizokui||WD=|lt=Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}} (みぞくいみみのみこと) – Grandfather of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (あめのひかたくしひかたのみこと) – Brother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō-no-Mikoto}} (すさのおのみこと) – Ancestor of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} (あめのこやねのみこと) – Ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|WD=Q641628}} === History === === Foundation === The date of foundation is unknown. According to shrine tradition, it was founded around the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html History and Sacred Treasures] (official website).. As noted in the section on enshrined deities, this shrine worships the lineage of {{ill|lt=Kamotake-tsunumi-no-Mikoto||WD=|lt=Mishima Mizokui Mimi}}, and its relationship with traditions found in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}} and {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} has been pointed out. In the Kojiki account of Emperor Jimmu, {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Kami}} falls in love with {{ill||lt=Seyadatara-hime|UNKNOWN}} (Tamaki-hime), the daughter of Mishima Mizokui, transforms into a vermilion-painted arrow, and strikes the maiden’s genitals. Though startled, she places the arrow by her bed, whereupon it turns into a handsome man, and from their union is born Futo-tatara Isuzu-ki-hime-no-Mikoto (Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto). In the Age of the Gods volume of the Nihon Shoki, it is written that Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was the child of the Ōmiwa deity, and that {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} transformed into an eight-fathom crocodile and visited Mishima Mizokui-hime (Tamaki-hime), producing Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, who later became the consort of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}. A similar account appears in the Nihon Shoki entry for the 16th day of the 8th month of the year of Emperor Jimmu’s accession, where Tamaki-hime is described as the daughter of Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto. Based on these texts, and noting that within the shrine precincts Kotoshironushi-no-Kami is enshrined rather than Ōmononushi-no-Kami, some theories point to interaction between the local earth-deity lineage of the {{ill||lt=Kamo clan|UNKNOWN}} that worshipped Kotoshironushi and the Mishima power group{{efn|Although Kotoshironushi is described as a child of Ōkuninushi of Izumo, one theory in *Nihon no Kamigami* suggests that Kotoshironushi was originally of Kamo lineage rather than Izumo.}}. In the surrounding area remain shrines such as {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} and {{ill|lt=Kamo Shrine (Takatsuki City)|Q11675604|WD=Q11675604|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, as well as place names like Kamomura and Kamobayashi, suggesting the influence of either the heavenly or earthly Kamo lineage. {{要出典範囲|There is also a theory that these interactions were later reconstructed into the myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, emphasizing Izumo deities as described above|date=November 2020}}. From the view that “mizo” symbolizes water and “mimi” represents length, another theory holds that this lineage was involved in irrigation related to the {{ill|lt=Ai River|Q11450512|WD=Q11450512}} system. === Historical outline === According to shrine tradition, the area once contained the {{ill|lt=field allotment|Q11381648|WD=Q11381648}} of {{ill|lt=Prince Abo|Q11657231|WD=Q11657231}}, son of {{ill|lt=Emperor Heijō|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}. During a drought in the {{ill|lt=Tencho|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}), the prince petitioned the court and received a mirror, with which he prayed for rain; heavy rain then fell“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The sacred treasure known as the “Dawn Sacred Mirror” (a bronze mirror with two deities and two beasts) is said to be that mirror. In the {{ill|lt=Engi Shiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed as “Mizokui Shrine, Shimashimo District, Settsu Province,” and is thus counted as a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}. In ancient times, there was an “Upper Shrine” about 500 meters to the northeast (present-day Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City) where Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was enshrined, while the current precincts were known as the “Lower Shrine,” enshrining Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto*Nihon no Kamigami*, entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The period when they were separated is unclear, but medieval documents from {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) mention place names of Upper Shrine and Lower Shrine. The Lower Shrine is considered the original Shikinaisha, and until their merger in Meiji 42, rituals were conducted at both shrines. Shiga Tsuyoshi, in *Research on Shikinaisha*, suggests that in ancient times the Lower Shrine’s Tamakushi-hime served the Upper Shrine’s Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, but after Isuzu-hime became Empress of Emperor Jimmu, the mother deity gained prominence and the Lower Shrine was elevated to Shikinaisha status. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunmei|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1477|Q6614|WD=Q6614}}) by the local lord Mizokui Hyōgo-no-suke Sadanobu, and again during the {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}} era ({{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769}}–{{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785}}) by Hasegawa Shikibu-shōyū. The current buildings date from {{ill|lt=Kanpō|Q1145798|WD=Q1145798}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1742|Q7575|WD=Q7575}}), constructed by Komeya Uemon and {{ill|lt=Sawano Tsuru|Q7428583|WD=Q7428583|lt=Komeya Kibei}}. Until the Meiji Restoration, the precincts also contained a Shingon temple, Fudō-in Myōren-ji, as the shrine-temple complex. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5), under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}, and in {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40) it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and paper offerings. In {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), three deities enshrined at Mizokui Upper Shrine east of the Ai River—Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto—were merged here. In the same year, the Tenman Shrine (Sugawara no Michizane) from Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the grounds, and in {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075}} (Meiji 43) the Arakami Shrine (Kagutsuchi-no-Kami, Nakatsuhiko-no-Kami, Nakatsuhime-no-Kami) from Megaki was also relocated to the precincts. In {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231}} (Shōwa 16), the shrine was promoted to {{ill||lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}} status. === Shrine grounds === File:Mizokui-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Mizokui-jinja haiden.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Mizokui-jinja entrance.JPG|Entrance to the grounds === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Mizokui 006.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Kotoshironushi Shrine]] === Associated shrines === * Kotoshironushi Shrine ** Enshrined deities: {{ill||lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami||WD=|lt=Kagu-tsuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill||lt=Okutsuhiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Okutsuhime-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Mitsuhanome-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Haniyasubiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Haniyasubime-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} :: Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto is the husband deity of Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto. During the Meiji period, the deities of Megaki Village were merged here. === Minor shrines === * Amaterasu Ōmikami Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill||lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Sarutahiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} * Uke-mochi Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Uke Mochi-no-Kami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}} * Tajikarao Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao||WD=|lt=Tajikarao-no-Kami}} * Konohana Sakuya-hime Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime||WD=|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{ill||lt=Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} On April 23, 1909 (Meiji 42), the Tenman Shrine of Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the Ichikishima Shrine. === Festivals === * Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Tondo Festival (January 15) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Inari Festival (second Sunday of April) * Ancestral Spirit Festival (Ryōsen Festival) (April 13 → April 11) – Noh performances were once dedicated. Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association * Arakami Festival (third Sunday of April) * Spring Grand Festival (May 5) * Autumn Grand Festival (October 14) * Shichi-Go-San (November 15) * Fire-Quelling Festival (December 10) * Great Purification Festival (December 31) In the past, based on the episode in the Nihon Shoki (Age of the Gods, upper volume) in which Kotoshironushi-no-Kami visited Mishima Mizokui-hime, a joint procession was held on the same day with Mishima Kamo Shrine, which also enshrines Kotoshironushi-no-Kami“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mishima Kamo Shrine.. === Sacred treasures === * Dawn Sacred Mirror According to shrine tradition, this mirror was used by Prince Abo during prayers for rain. It is a bronze mirror from China’s Han dynasty, decorated with two deities and two beasts. === Excavated pottery === In 1939 (Shōwa 14), during river improvement works on the Ai River, large quantities of {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}} and {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}} were excavated from land adjacent to the former Mizokui Upper Shrine, and records show they were kept at Mizokui ShrineAmbo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association. The site of the former Upper Shrine and its surroundings constitute the “'''Mizokui Archaeological Site'''.” In excavations conducted in 1997 (Heisei 9) by the {{ill|lt=Public Interest Incorporated Foundation|Q40734470|WD=Q40734470}} {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center|Q11425913|WD=Q11425913}} (formerly the Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Research Center), remains were discovered including {{ill|lt=small-plot paddy fields|Q86743587|WD=Q86743587}} from the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}}, artifacts from the {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} and {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and square-section structural remains believed to be the former Mizokui Upper Shrine from the latter half of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}[https://www.occh.or.jp/?s=data/setumeikai Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center] 1997. === Related sites === [[File:Mizokui 101.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Monument marking the former Mizokui Upper Shrine]] * Former site of Mizokui Upper Shrine (Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City, {{ウィキ座標|34|49|02.64|N|135|35|25.28|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|location|name=Upper Shrine Site (monument)}}) : The site of the former Upper Shrine, marked by a stone monument. It was erected in April 1965 (Shōwa 40) when Naniwa Commercial High School built an athletic field on the site. * {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} : A shrine in Takatsuki City located across the Ai River. The crest of Mishima Kamo Shrine can be seen on stone lanterns within Mizokui Shrine’s grounds. According to tradition, portable shrine processions from Mishima Kamo Shrine to Mizokui Shrine began in Genroku 10 (1697) and continued until the Meiji period. === Visitor information === ; Address * 9-21 Isuzuchō, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, Osaka Prefecture ; Access * From {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Station|Q1072931|WD=Q1072931}} ({{ill|lt=West Japan Railway First torii gate |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} 9-21 Isuzuchō |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|48|46.61|N|135|35|19.16|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizokui Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|WD=Q5353793|lt=Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = |Other names = |Annual festivals = {{ill|lt=May 5|Q2550|WD=Q2550}} (Spring Festival)
{{ill|lt=October 14|Q2922|WD=Q2922}} (Autumn Festival) |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Mizokui Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} {{Coordinate list}} '''Mizokui Shrine''' (みぞくいじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}}. It is an independent shrine. Due to damage from the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=2018 Northern Osaka Earthquake|UNKNOWN}}, the protective buildings of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=subsidiary shrines|UNKNOWN}} were rebuilt in a modern style. The former chief priest’s residence adjacent to the shrine was removed, and the shrine office was also rebuilt. On the 1st and 2nd of each month, amulets are distributed, and thanks to the efforts of the parishioners, the shrine’s rituals continue to be maintained. === Overview === Enshrined deities
Simplified diagram of relationships among the enshrined deities
Solid lines indicate direct children; dotted vertical lines indicate abbreviated multiple generations; dotted horizontal lines indicate marriage relationships; numbers indicate the order of imperial accession. Bold text indicates deities enshrined at this shrine, and red text indicates female figures. All are listed according to the official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html]. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:90%}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01| 01='''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}'''|02={{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|03={{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|04=}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||:}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01||||02| 01={{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{ill|lt=Mishima Mizokui||WD=|lt=Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||!|||||!}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01|y|~|~|02| 01={{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|{{color|#FC4E6B|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0||||||,|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|border=0|||||01||!|||!| 01='''Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto'''}} {{familytree|border=0|01|y|~|~|~|02||!| 01=(1) {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}|02='''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|{{color|#FC4E6B|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||01|y|~|~|~|~|~|02| 01=(2) {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321}}|02='''{{jalink|Isuzu-yori-hime|Q10881557|{{color|#FC4E6B|Isuzu Yorihime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree/end}}
The current enshrined deities are the following six kamiThe list of enshrined deities follows the official website’s “Shrine Grounds Guide” [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129210623/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub1.html].. ; Principal deities * '''{{ill|lt=Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}''' (たまくしひめのみこと) – Mother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * '''{{ill|lt=Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|WD=Q5353793|lt=Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと) – Empress of Emperor Jimmu ; Co-enshrined deities * {{ill|lt=Mishima Mizokui||WD=|lt=Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}} (みぞくいみみのみこと) – Grandfather of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (あめのひかたくしひかたのみこと) – Brother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō-no-Mikoto}} (すさのおのみこと) – Ancestor of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} (あめのこやねのみこと) – Ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|WD=Q641628}} === History === === Foundation === The date of foundation is unknown. According to shrine tradition, it was founded around the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html History and Sacred Treasures] (official website).. As noted in the section on enshrined deities, this shrine worships the lineage of {{ill|lt=Kamotake-tsunumi-no-Mikoto||WD=|lt=Mishima Mizokui Mimi}}, and its relationship with traditions found in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}} and {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} has been pointed out. In the Kojiki account of Emperor Jimmu, {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Kami}} falls in love with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Seyadatara-hime|UNKNOWN}} (Tamaki-hime), the daughter of Mishima Mizokui, transforms into a vermilion-painted arrow, and strikes the maiden’s genitals. Though startled, she places the arrow by her bed, whereupon it turns into a handsome man, and from their union is born Futo-tatara Isuzu-ki-hime-no-Mikoto (Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto). In the Age of the Gods volume of the Nihon Shoki, it is written that Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was the child of the Ōmiwa deity, and that {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} transformed into an eight-fathom crocodile and visited Mishima Mizokui-hime (Tamaki-hime), producing Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, who later became the consort of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}. A similar account appears in the Nihon Shoki entry for the 16th day of the 8th month of the year of Emperor Jimmu’s accession, where Tamaki-hime is described as the daughter of Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto. Based on these texts, and noting that within the shrine precincts Kotoshironushi-no-Kami is enshrined rather than Ōmononushi-no-Kami, some theories point to interaction between the local earth-deity lineage of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamo clan|UNKNOWN}} that worshipped Kotoshironushi and the Mishima power group{{efn|Although Kotoshironushi is described as a child of Ōkuninushi of Izumo, one theory in *Nihon no Kamigami* suggests that Kotoshironushi was originally of Kamo lineage rather than Izumo.}}. In the surrounding area remain shrines such as {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} and {{ill|lt=Kamo Shrine (Takatsuki City)|Q11675604|WD=Q11675604|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, as well as place names like Kamomura and Kamobayashi, suggesting the influence of either the heavenly or earthly Kamo lineage. {{要出典範囲|There is also a theory that these interactions were later reconstructed into the myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, emphasizing Izumo deities as described above|date=November 2020}}. From the view that “mizo” symbolizes water and “mimi” represents length, another theory holds that this lineage was involved in irrigation related to the {{ill|lt=Ai River|Q11450512|WD=Q11450512}} system. === Historical outline === According to shrine tradition, the area once contained the {{ill|lt=field allotment|Q11381648|WD=Q11381648}} of {{ill|lt=Prince Abo|Q11657231|WD=Q11657231}}, son of {{ill|lt=Emperor Heijō|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}. During a drought in the {{ill|lt=Tencho|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}), the prince petitioned the court and received a mirror, with which he prayed for rain; heavy rain then fell“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The sacred treasure known as the “Dawn Sacred Mirror” (a bronze mirror with two deities and two beasts) is said to be that mirror. In the {{ill|lt=Engi Shiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed as “Mizokui Shrine, Shimashimo District, Settsu Province,” and is thus counted as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}. In ancient times, there was an “Upper Shrine” about 500 meters to the northeast (present-day Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City) where Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was enshrined, while the current precincts were known as the “Lower Shrine,” enshrining Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto*Nihon no Kamigami*, entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The period when they were separated is unclear, but medieval documents from {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) mention place names of Upper Shrine and Lower Shrine. The Lower Shrine is considered the original Shikinaisha, and until their merger in Meiji 42, rituals were conducted at both shrines. Shiga Tsuyoshi, in *Research on Shikinaisha*, suggests that in ancient times the Lower Shrine’s Tamakushi-hime served the Upper Shrine’s Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, but after Isuzu-hime became Empress of Emperor Jimmu, the mother deity gained prominence and the Lower Shrine was elevated to Shikinaisha status. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunmei|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1477|Q6614|WD=Q6614}}) by the local lord Mizokui Hyōgo-no-suke Sadanobu, and again during the {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}} era ({{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769}}–{{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785}}) by Hasegawa Shikibu-shōyū. The current buildings date from {{ill|lt=Kanpō|Q1145798|WD=Q1145798}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1742|Q7575|WD=Q7575}}), constructed by Komeya Uemon and {{ill|lt=Sawano Tsuru|Q7428583|WD=Q7428583|lt=Komeya Kibei}}. Until the Meiji Restoration, the precincts also contained a Shingon temple, Fudō-in Myōren-ji, as the shrine-temple complex. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5), under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}, and in {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40) it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and paper offerings. In {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), three deities enshrined at Mizokui Upper Shrine east of the Ai River—Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto—were merged here. In the same year, the Tenman Shrine (Sugawara no Michizane) from Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the grounds, and in {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075}} (Meiji 43) the Arakami Shrine (Kagutsuchi-no-Kami, Nakatsuhiko-no-Kami, Nakatsuhime-no-Kami) from Megaki was also relocated to the precincts. In {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231}} (Shōwa 16), the shrine was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}} status. === Shrine grounds === File:Mizokui-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Mizokui-jinja haiden.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Mizokui-jinja entrance.JPG|Entrance to the grounds === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Mizokui 006.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Kotoshironushi Shrine]] === Associated shrines === * Kotoshironushi Shrine ** Enshrined deities: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami||WD=|lt=Kagu-tsuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Okutsuhiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Okutsuhime-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuhanome-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Haniyasubiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Haniyasubime-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} :: Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto is the husband deity of Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto. During the Meiji period, the deities of Megaki Village were merged here. === Minor shrines === * Amaterasu Ōmikami Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} * Uke-mochi Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Uke Mochi-no-Kami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}} * Tajikarao Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao||WD=|lt=Tajikarao-no-Kami}} * Konohana Sakuya-hime Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime||WD=|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} On April 23, 1909 (Meiji 42), the Tenman Shrine of Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the Ichikishima Shrine. === Festivals === * Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Tondo Festival (January 15) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Inari Festival (second Sunday of April) * Ancestral Spirit Festival (Ryōsen Festival) (April 13 → April 11) – Noh performances were once dedicated. Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association * Arakami Festival (third Sunday of April) * Spring Grand Festival (May 5) * Autumn Grand Festival (October 14) * Shichi-Go-San (November 15) * Fire-Quelling Festival (December 10) * Great Purification Festival (December 31) In the past, based on the episode in the Nihon Shoki (Age of the Gods, upper volume) in which Kotoshironushi-no-Kami visited Mishima Mizokui-hime, a joint procession was held on the same day with Mishima Kamo Shrine, which also enshrines Kotoshironushi-no-Kami“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mishima Kamo Shrine.. === Sacred treasures === * Dawn Sacred Mirror According to shrine tradition, this mirror was used by Prince Abo during prayers for rain. It is a bronze mirror from China’s Han dynasty, decorated with two deities and two beasts. === Excavated pottery === In 1939 (Shōwa 14), during river improvement works on the Ai River, large quantities of {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}} and {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}} were excavated from land adjacent to the former Mizokui Upper Shrine, and records show they were kept at Mizokui ShrineAmbo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association. The site of the former Upper Shrine and its surroundings constitute the “'''Mizokui Archaeological Site'''.” In excavations conducted in 1997 (Heisei 9) by the {{ill|lt=Public Interest Incorporated Foundation|Q40734470|WD=Q40734470}} {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center|Q11425913|WD=Q11425913}} (formerly the Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Research Center), remains were discovered including {{ill|lt=small-plot paddy fields|Q86743587|WD=Q86743587}} from the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}}, artifacts from the {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} and {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and square-section structural remains believed to be the former Mizokui Upper Shrine from the latter half of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}[https://www.occh.or.jp/?s=data/setumeikai Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center] 1997. === Related sites === [[File:Mizokui 101.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Monument marking the former Mizokui Upper Shrine]] * Former site of Mizokui Upper Shrine (Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City, {{ウィキ座標|34|49|02.64|N|135|35|25.28|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|location|name=Upper Shrine Site (monument)}}) : The site of the former Upper Shrine, marked by a stone monument. It was erected in April 1965 (Shōwa 40) when Naniwa Commercial High School built an athletic field on the site. * {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} : A shrine in Takatsuki City located across the Ai River. The crest of Mishima Kamo Shrine can be seen on stone lanterns within Mizokui Shrine’s grounds. According to tradition, portable shrine processions from Mishima Kamo Shrine to Mizokui Shrine began in Genroku 10 (1697) and continued until the Meiji period. === Visitor information === ; Address * 9-21 Isuzuchō, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, Osaka Prefecture ; Access * From {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Station|Q1072931|WD=Q1072931}} ({{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}) East Exit or {{ill|lt=Ibaraki-shi Station|Q3969806|WD=Q3969806}} ({{ill|lt=Hankyu Corporation|Q473058|WD=Q473058}}) South Exit, take the {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Bus|Q18636319|WD=Q18636319}} (Route 84 Kuwata–Hirata Loop) and get off at “Kuwata-chō” bus stop; about a 4-minute walk east after disembarking. === Notes === ; Annotations ; References {{reflist}} === name in kana (P1814) == * みぞくいじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7120905000159 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '大阪府茨木市五十鈴町9-21', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:溝咋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11564491}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizokui Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizokui-jinja torii.JPG|250px]]
First torii gate |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} 9-21 Isuzuchō |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|48|46.61|N|135|35|19.16|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizokui Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|WD=Q5353793|lt=Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = |Other names = |Annual festivals = {{ill|lt=May 5|Q2550|WD=Q2550}} (Spring Festival)
{{ill|lt=October 14|Q2922|WD=Q2922}} (Autumn Festival) |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Mizokui Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} {{Coordinate list}} '''Mizokui Shrine''' (みぞくいじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is an independent shrine. Due to damage from the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=2018 Northern Osaka Earthquake|1=UNKNOWN}}, the protective buildings of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=subsidiary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}} were rebuilt in a modern style. The former chief priest’s residence adjacent to the shrine was removed, and the shrine office was also rebuilt. On the 1st and 2nd of each month, amulets are distributed, and thanks to the efforts of the parishioners, the shrine’s rituals continue to be maintained. === Overview === Enshrined deities
Simplified diagram of relationships among the enshrined deities
Solid lines indicate direct children; dotted vertical lines indicate abbreviated multiple generations; dotted horizontal lines indicate marriage relationships; numbers indicate the order of imperial accession. Bold text indicates deities enshrined at this shrine, and red text indicates female figures. All are listed according to the official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html]. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:90%}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01| 01='''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}'''|02={{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|03={{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|04=}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||:}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01||||02| 01={{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{ill|lt=Mishima Mizokui||WD=|lt=Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||||||!|||||!}} {{familytree|border=0||||||01|y|~|~|02| 01={{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|02='''{{jalink|Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|{{color|#FC4E6B|Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0||||||,|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|border=0|||||01||!|||!| 01='''Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto'''}} {{familytree|border=0|01|y|~|~|~|02||!| 01=(1) {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}|02='''{{jalink|Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|{{color|#FC4E6B|Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree|border=0|||01|y|~|~|~|~|~|02| 01=(2) {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321}}|02='''{{jalink|Isuzu-yori-hime|Q10881557|{{color|#FC4E6B|Isuzu Yorihime-no-Mikoto}}}}'''}} {{familytree/end}}
The current enshrined deities are the following six kamiThe list of enshrined deities follows the official website’s “Shrine Grounds Guide” [https://web.archive.org/web/20160129210623/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub1.html].. ; Principal deities * '''{{ill|lt=Tamaki-hime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto}}''' (たまくしひめのみこと) – Mother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * '''{{ill|lt=Himetatara-isuzuhime|Q5353793|WD=Q5353793|lt=Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと) – Empress of Emperor Jimmu ; Co-enshrined deities * {{ill|lt=Mishima Mizokui||WD=|lt=Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto}} (みぞくいみみのみこと) – Grandfather of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あめのひかたくしひかたのみこと) – Brother of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō-no-Mikoto}} (すさのおのみこと) – Ancestor of Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} (あめのこやねのみこと) – Ancestor of the {{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|WD=Q641628}} === History === === Foundation === The date of foundation is unknown. According to shrine tradition, it was founded around the reign of the 10th emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20160129181104/http://www.geocities.jp/mizokuijinja/sub2.html History and Sacred Treasures] (official website).. As noted in the section on enshrined deities, this shrine worships the lineage of {{ill|lt=Kamotake-tsunumi-no-Mikoto||WD=|lt=Mishima Mizokui Mimi}}, and its relationship with traditions found in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}} and {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} has been pointed out. In the Kojiki account of Emperor Jimmu, {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Kami}} falls in love with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Seyadatara-hime|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tamaki-hime), the daughter of Mishima Mizokui, transforms into a vermilion-painted arrow, and strikes the maiden’s genitals. Though startled, she places the arrow by her bed, whereupon it turns into a handsome man, and from their union is born Futo-tatara Isuzu-ki-hime-no-Mikoto (Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto). In the Age of the Gods volume of the Nihon Shoki, it is written that Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was the child of the Ōmiwa deity, and that {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} transformed into an eight-fathom crocodile and visited Mishima Mizokui-hime (Tamaki-hime), producing Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, who later became the consort of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}. A similar account appears in the Nihon Shoki entry for the 16th day of the 8th month of the year of Emperor Jimmu’s accession, where Tamaki-hime is described as the daughter of Mishima Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto. Based on these texts, and noting that within the shrine precincts Kotoshironushi-no-Kami is enshrined rather than Ōmononushi-no-Kami, some theories point to interaction between the local earth-deity lineage of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamo clan|1=UNKNOWN}} that worshipped Kotoshironushi and the Mishima power group{{efn|Although Kotoshironushi is described as a child of Ōkuninushi of Izumo, one theory in *Nihon no Kamigami* suggests that Kotoshironushi was originally of Kamo lineage rather than Izumo.}}. In the surrounding area remain shrines such as {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} and {{ill|lt=Kamo Shrine (Takatsuki City)|Q11675604|WD=Q11675604|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, as well as place names like Kamomura and Kamobayashi, suggesting the influence of either the heavenly or earthly Kamo lineage. {{要出典範囲|There is also a theory that these interactions were later reconstructed into the myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, emphasizing Izumo deities as described above|date=November 2020}}. From the view that “mizo” symbolizes water and “mimi” represents length, another theory holds that this lineage was involved in irrigation related to the {{ill|lt=Ai River|Q11450512|WD=Q11450512}} system. === Historical outline === According to shrine tradition, the area once contained the {{ill|lt=field allotment|Q11381648|WD=Q11381648}} of {{ill|lt=Prince Abo|Q11657231|WD=Q11657231}}, son of {{ill|lt=Emperor Heijō|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}. During a drought in the {{ill|lt=Tencho|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}), the prince petitioned the court and received a mirror, with which he prayed for rain; heavy rain then fell“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The sacred treasure known as the “Dawn Sacred Mirror” (a bronze mirror with two deities and two beasts) is said to be that mirror. In the {{ill|lt=Engi Shiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed as “Mizokui Shrine, Shimashimo District, Settsu Province,” and is thus counted as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}. In ancient times, there was an “Upper Shrine” about 500 meters to the northeast (present-day Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City) where Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto was enshrined, while the current precincts were known as the “Lower Shrine,” enshrining Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto*Nihon no Kamigami*, entry on Mizokui Shrine.. The period when they were separated is unclear, but medieval documents from {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) mention place names of Upper Shrine and Lower Shrine. The Lower Shrine is considered the original Shikinaisha, and until their merger in Meiji 42, rituals were conducted at both shrines. Shiga Tsuyoshi, in *Research on Shikinaisha*, suggests that in ancient times the Lower Shrine’s Tamakushi-hime served the Upper Shrine’s Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, but after Isuzu-hime became Empress of Emperor Jimmu, the mother deity gained prominence and the Lower Shrine was elevated to Shikinaisha status. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunmei|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1477|Q6614|WD=Q6614}}) by the local lord Mizokui Hyōgo-no-suke Sadanobu, and again during the {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|WD=Q1373039}} era ({{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769}}–{{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|WD=Q6785}}) by Hasegawa Shikibu-shōyū. The current buildings date from {{ill|lt=Kanpō|Q1145798|WD=Q1145798}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1742|Q7575|WD=Q7575}}), constructed by Komeya Uemon and {{ill|lt=Sawano Tsuru|Q7428583|WD=Q7428583|lt=Komeya Kibei}}. Until the Meiji Restoration, the precincts also contained a Shingon temple, Fudō-in Myōren-ji, as the shrine-temple complex. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5), under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}, and in {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40) it was designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and paper offerings. In {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), three deities enshrined at Mizokui Upper Shrine east of the Ai River—Himetatara Isuzuhime-no-Mikoto, Mizokui Mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ame-no-Hikata-Kushi-Hikata-no-Mikoto—were merged here. In the same year, the Tenman Shrine (Sugawara no Michizane) from Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the grounds, and in {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075}} (Meiji 43) the Arakami Shrine (Kagutsuchi-no-Kami, Nakatsuhiko-no-Kami, Nakatsuhime-no-Kami) from Megaki was also relocated to the precincts. In {{ill|lt=1941|Q5231|WD=Q5231}} (Shōwa 16), the shrine was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|1=UNKNOWN}} status. === Shrine grounds === File:Mizokui-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Mizokui-jinja haiden.JPG|Shrine grounds File:Mizokui-jinja entrance.JPG|Entrance to the grounds === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Mizokui 006.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Kotoshironushi Shrine]] === Associated shrines === * Kotoshironushi Shrine ** Enshrined deities: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami||WD=|lt=Kagu-tsuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Okutsuhiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Okutsuhime-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuhanome-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Haniyasubiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Haniyasubime-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} :: Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto is the husband deity of Tamaki-hime-no-Mikoto. During the Meiji period, the deities of Megaki Village were merged here. === Minor shrines === * Amaterasu Ōmikami Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} * Uke-mochi Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Uke Mochi-no-Kami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}} * Tajikarao Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao||WD=|lt=Tajikarao-no-Kami}} * Konohana Sakuya-hime Shrine – Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime||WD=|lt=Konohana Sakuya-hime-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko-no-Kami}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} On April 23, 1909 (Meiji 42), the Tenman Shrine of Nikaidō Hoshimi was merged into the Ichikishima Shrine. === Festivals === * Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Tondo Festival (January 15) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Inari Festival (second Sunday of April) * Ancestral Spirit Festival (Ryōsen Festival) (April 13 → April 11) – Noh performances were once dedicated. Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association * Arakami Festival (third Sunday of April) * Spring Grand Festival (May 5) * Autumn Grand Festival (October 14) * Shichi-Go-San (November 15) * Fire-Quelling Festival (December 10) * Great Purification Festival (December 31) In the past, based on the episode in the Nihon Shoki (Age of the Gods, upper volume) in which Kotoshironushi-no-Kami visited Mishima Mizokui-hime, a joint procession was held on the same day with Mishima Kamo Shrine, which also enshrines Kotoshironushi-no-Kami“Osaka Prefecture Place Names,” entry on Mishima Kamo Shrine.. === Sacred treasures === * Dawn Sacred Mirror According to shrine tradition, this mirror was used by Prince Abo during prayers for rain. It is a bronze mirror from China’s Han dynasty, decorated with two deities and two beasts. === Excavated pottery === In 1939 (Shōwa 14), during river improvement works on the Ai River, large quantities of {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}} and {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}} were excavated from land adjacent to the former Mizokui Upper Shrine, and records show they were kept at Mizokui ShrineAmbo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association. The site of the former Upper Shrine and its surroundings constitute the “'''Mizokui Archaeological Site'''.” In excavations conducted in 1997 (Heisei 9) by the {{ill|lt=Public Interest Incorporated Foundation|Q40734470|WD=Q40734470}} {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center|Q11425913|WD=Q11425913}} (formerly the Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Research Center), remains were discovered including {{ill|lt=small-plot paddy fields|Q86743587|WD=Q86743587}} from the {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}}, artifacts from the {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} and {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and square-section structural remains believed to be the former Mizokui Upper Shrine from the latter half of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}[https://www.occh.or.jp/?s=data/setumeikai Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center] 1997. === Related sites === [[File:Mizokui 101.JPG|200px|thumb|right|Monument marking the former Mizokui Upper Shrine]] * Former site of Mizokui Upper Shrine (Gakuenchō, Ibaraki City, {{ウィキ座標|34|49|02.64|N|135|35|25.28|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|location|name=Upper Shrine Site (monument)}}) : The site of the former Upper Shrine, marked by a stone monument. It was erected in April 1965 (Shōwa 40) when Naniwa Commercial High School built an athletic field on the site. * {{ill|lt=Mishima Kamo Shrine|Q11355295|WD=Q11355295}} : A shrine in Takatsuki City located across the Ai River. The crest of Mishima Kamo Shrine can be seen on stone lanterns within Mizokui Shrine’s grounds. According to tradition, portable shrine processions from Mishima Kamo Shrine to Mizokui Shrine began in Genroku 10 (1697) and continued until the Meiji period. === Visitor information === ; Address * 9-21 Isuzuchō, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, Osaka Prefecture ; Access * From {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Station|Q1072931|WD=Q1072931}} ({{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}) East Exit or {{ill|lt=Ibaraki-shi Station|Q3969806|WD=Q3969806}} ({{ill|lt=Hankyu Corporation|Q473058|WD=Q473058}}) South Exit, take the {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Bus|Q18636319|WD=Q18636319}} (Route 84 Kuwata–Hirata Loop) and get off at “Kuwata-chō” bus stop; about a 4-minute walk east after disembarking. === Notes === ; Annotations ; References {{reflist}} === Further reading === * Inoue Masao, *Complete Gazetteer of Osaka Prefecture* (1922) {{National Diet Library Bib ID|73012615}} * Ambo Yukihiko, “Historic Sites and Scenic Places of Mishima District” (1961), included in *Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments of Osaka Prefecture: Mishima District* (1974) {{National Diet Library Bib ID|73009804}} * Ambo Yukihiko, “History of Mizokui Shrine,” Mizokui Shrine Support Association (compiled in the Meiji period to elevate the shrine’s rank) * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Our Town Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National Diet Library Bib ID|89049621}} * Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties Center, *[https://www.occh.or.jp/static/pdf/data/setumeikai/h09_024.pdf Mizokui Archaeological Site]* (Excavation site briefing materials {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=pdf|1=UNKNOWN}}), April 12, 1997 * Miyoshi Teiji, *Compilation of Osaka Traditional Geography* (2008) ISBN 978-4-7924-0647-9 {{National Diet Library Bib ID|21487934}} * *{{ill|lt=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Place Names|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), entry on Mizokui Shrine in Ibaraki City * Matsushita Akira, “Mizokui Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 3: Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === {{Expand Japanese|溝咋神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Mizokui Shrine | native_name = 溝咋神社 | image = Mizokui-jinja torii.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Mizokui Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|34.812829|135.588656}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|lt=Tamakushi-hime|en|Tamakushi-hime|ja|玉櫛媛|zh|玉依媛|WD=Q11572681}} }} {{nihongo|'''Mizokui Shrine'''|溝咋神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Settsu Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická
